current_fact.fact_text: In 1954, the Geneva Conference opened as diplomats from major powers gathered to seek an end to the wars in Indochina and to address tensions on the Korean Peninsula. Around the negotiating tables, maps were redrawn and ceasefires debated, showing how much the fate of entire regions can hinge on words spoken in a single city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

current_fact.match_reason: A high‑stakes diplomatic conference and the evolution of flamenco both show how history is negotiated—one through treaties, the other through centuries of cultural dialogue and reinvention.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1944, Georgios Papandreou became head of the Greek government‑in‑exile based in Egypt during World War II. Governing a country from afar, he had to lead a scattered nation through occupation, resistance, and the hope of eventual return.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A Greek leadership exiled across the Mediterranean to Egypt resonates with an episode sailing musically around the same sea, linking scattered shores and identities.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1944, British commandos and Cretan resistance fighters kidnapped German General Heinrich Kreipe in occupied Crete. They famously smuggled him over the island’s rugged mountains under the noses of Nazi patrols, an operation so audacious it later inspired films and legends.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A daring mountain escape in Crete aligns with an episode shaped by mountain landscapes and the stories that echo through high, rugged terrain.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1937, the small Basque town of Guernica was bombed by the German Condor Legion and Italian Aviazione Legionaria during the Spanish Civil War. The attack, one of the first aerial bombings of a civilian population, shocked the world and inspired Pablo Picasso’s haunting anti‑war masterpiece, ‘Guernica.’

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: An infamous bombing that became a symbol of anti‑war protest matches an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music channels political outrage and resistance.

queue.fact_text: In 1805, during the First Barbary War, U.S. Marines and mercenaries captured the Libyan port city of Derna. The daring overland march to raise the American flag on foreign soil—immortalized in the ‘Marines’ Hymn’—marked one of the earliest overseas military actions by the young United States.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A coastal campaign across Mediterranean waters maps onto a seafaring episode about life on a boat, shared journeys, and navigating distant shores.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1794, the Battle of Beaumont unfolded during the Flanders Campaign of the War of the First Coalition. It was one of many swirling clashes where revolutionary France and Europe’s monarchies tested new tactics, alliances, and the rapidly changing shape of warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A battlefield where power dynamics are contested contrasts with a dancefloor where space and power are reclaimed—especially by women—turning struggle into movement instead of bloodshed.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1945, the Battle of Bautzen saw one of the last successful German tank offensives of World War II. Even as the Third Reich was collapsing, this grim, late victory by the Wehrmacht underscored how chaotic and deadly the final days of the war remained on the Eastern Front.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A mechanized battle in the war’s final days mirrors, in a transformed way, the disciplined combat of Muay Thai—violence channeled into ritual, technique, and personal growth instead of destruction.

queue.fact_text: In 1923, the Duke of York—future King George VI—married Lady Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon at Westminster Abbey. She broke with tradition by laying her bouquet on the Tomb of the Unknown Warrior, a gesture of remembrance that turned a royal wedding into a small act of national mourning and healing after World War I.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A royal love story with a tender, symbolic gesture fits an episode devoted to love’s vulnerability and the emotional weight behind romantic rituals.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1964, Tanganyika and Zanzibar merged to form the United Republic of Tanzania. The new country’s name blended both predecessors, symbolizing a political and cultural union that stretched from the East African mainland to the spice-rich islands of the Indian Ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: The creation of Tanzania as a new African union aligns with an episode celebrating Afrobeat and pan‑African musical currents that cross borders and blend identities.

queue.fact_text: In 1802, Napoleon Bonaparte issued a general amnesty allowing most émigrés of the French Revolution to return home. Nearly a decade after many had fled guillotines and chaos, the decree invited exiles back to a transformed France—though the most outspoken opponents were still kept at a distance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

queue.match_reason: An amnesty that lets exiles come back to their homeland resonates with an episode about reunion under one roof and the warmth of finally feeling at home again.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1994, South Africa began its first multiracial general election, ending decades of apartheid rule. Millions of people who had never been allowed to vote lined up for hours, and the African National Congress, led by Nelson Mandela, won—turning a ballot into a global symbol of reconciliation and new beginnings.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A historic political ‘first’ that reshaped a nation pairs with Sedna FM’s own first episode—a starting point that set the tone for everything that followed, including Afrobeat’s spirit of resistance.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 2025, a car-ramming attack during a Lapu-Lapu Day festival in Vancouver killed 11 people and injured at least 30. The celebration was meant to honor a Filipino hero who resisted colonial invasion, turning the tragedy into a stark reminder of how public spaces of joy can be violently disrupted.

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

queue.match_reason: A violent attack in a public festivity confronts us with the fragility of crowded events; JOMO’s invitation to step back from constant social presence offers a quieter counterpoint and a way to rethink how and when we gather.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1802, the same amnesty that let many émigrés return also highlighted a curious detail: roughly one thousand of the ‘most notorious’ opponents of the regime were still excluded. Even reconciliation, it seemed, came with carefully drawn red lines and a political edit of who was allowed back into the story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s selective amnesty ‘edits’ who gets to return, just as Auto‑Tune edits which voices are acceptable—both raising questions about control, correction, and who is left outside the official mix.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1962, NASA’s Ranger 4 crash wasn’t the only cosmic story of the day: together with Ariel 1 and earlier Soviet efforts, it marked a period when space was becoming a laboratory rather than a distant dream. In just a few years, the Moon went from mythic lantern to an object being hit, measured, and mapped by small, fragile machines from Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: As we turned the Moon into an object for instruments, we began weaving technology tightly into our lives; an episode about our dependence on devices echoes that expanding web from space probes to smartphones.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1803, when the L’Aigle meteorites fell, some locals reportedly thought a volcano had erupted in the sky. The meticulous investigation that followed—counting fragments over dozens of square kilometers—helped transform meteorites from folklore and fear into data and science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A sky once filled with fearsome ‘fire’ is now understood calmly; reggae and dub’s spacious, earth‑rooted rhythms offer that same grounding after cosmic surprise—like waking in the woods under a quiet, mysterious sky.

queue.fact_text: On April 26, 1964, as Tanzania was born, its leaders promoted ‘Ujamaa’—a vision of African socialism centered on community and shared growth. The new nation’s story became one of weaving together different ethnicities, languages, and island–mainland cultures into a single, evolving identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Forging a new country from many parts is a collective act of learning and adaptation, echoing the episode’s focus on slow progress, effort, and the gradual joy of building something enduring together.

published.fact_text: In 1962, NASA’s Ranger 4 became the first U.S. spacecraft to reach the Moon’s surface—by accident. A technical failure left it ‘dead’ in space, so it slammed into the far side of the Moon without sending back any scientific data, turning a planned triumph into a strangely pioneering crash landing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A Moon probe that turned failure into a first pairs well with an episode about rethinking emptiness as creative space and shifting perspective.

published.fact_text: On April 26, 1962, the United Kingdom entered the Space Age with the launch of Ariel 1, its first satellite. Built in collaboration with NASA, the tiny craft focused on studying Earth’s upper atmosphere and cosmic rays, quietly turning Britain into the third country to operate its own satellite.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: Ariel 1’s launch as a national leap into orbit mirrors the episode’s theme of setting off toward new destinations and sonic territories.

published.fact_text: In 1993, Space Shuttle Columbia’s STS-55 mission turned the orbiter into an orbiting laboratory with the Spacelab module. For almost 10 days, astronauts ran more than 80 experiments in microgravity, turning weightlessness into a tool for biology, materials science, and the physics of life in space.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: A shuttle turned into a lab in orbit fits the idea of a new format designed as a free-floating ‘space’ for experiments in sound and mood.

published.fact_text: On April 26, 1803, thousands of meteorite fragments rained down near L’Aigle in Normandy, France. The event was so spectacular and well-documented that it finally convinced many skeptical European scientists that stones really can fall from the sky, reshaping our understanding of the solar system.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A sky full of falling stones and an episode opening with ‘Solar Wind’ both play with cosmic imagery and the sense of invisible forces reshaping our world.

published.fact_text: In 1986, a late-night safety test at the Chernobyl nuclear power plant went catastrophically wrong, causing the worst nuclear power accident in history. The explosion and fire released vast amounts of radioactive material, turning an ordinary Soviet town into an eerie, long-term laboratory for ecology, health, and resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: While Chernobyl is a tragic example of technological hubris, its long-term consequences invite reflection on imperfection, unintended outcomes, and learning from mistakes—resonating with the episode’s meditation on flaws and ‘happy accidents.’

published.fact_text: On April 26, 1721, a massive earthquake devastated the Iranian city of Tabriz. The quake leveled large parts of this historic Silk Road hub, reminding contemporaries that even long‑lived centers of trade and culture can be reshaped in a single violent moment by the Earth beneath them.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An earthquake that violently shakes a city contrasts with a ‘slow planet’—the pairing invites a contemplative, healing tempo after sudden geological upheaval.

published.fact_text: In 1966, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake struck Tashkent, then the largest city in Soviet Central Asia, with an intensity strong enough to destroy most of the city. Although thousands were left homeless, the disaster triggered one of the Soviet Union’s most ambitious urban rebuilding projects, giving Tashkent a new, modern face.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A city rebuilt from rubble fits with an episode about weathering storms and trusting that harsh conditions—earthquakes or rain—do not last forever.

published.fact_text: On April 26, 1991, a violent tornado outbreak unleashed 55 tornadoes across the central United States. One of them, the Andover, Kansas tornado, reached the rare F5 intensity, scouring homes from their foundations and becoming one of the most-studied twisters for understanding the extreme power of nature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A ferocious tornado outbreak is nature’s own ‘electric shock,’ and the high‑voltage rock energy mirrors the raw, charged atmosphere of a storm‑torn sky.

published.fact_text: In 1954, the Geneva Conference opened as diplomats from major powers gathered to seek an end to the wars in Indochina and to address tensions on the Korean Peninsula. Around the negotiating tables, maps were redrawn and ceasefires debated, showing how much the fate of entire regions can hinge on words spoken in a single city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A high‑stakes diplomatic conference and the evolution of flamenco both show how history is negotiated—one through treaties, the other through centuries of cultural dialogue and reinvention.
